## DENOISING DIFFUSION IMPLICIT MODELS

## **Jiaming Song, Chenlin Meng & Stefano Ermon**

Stanford University

_{_ tsong,chenlin,ermon _}_ @cs.stanford.edu

## ABSTRACT

Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) have achieved high quality image generation without adversarial training, yet they require simulating a Markov chain for many steps in order to produce a sample. To accelerate sampling, we present denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIMs), a more efficient class of iterative implicit probabilistic models with the same training procedure as DDPMs. In DDPMs, the generative process is defined as the reverse of a particular Markovian diffusion process. We generalize DDPMs via a class of non-Markovian diffusion processes that lead to the same training objective. These non-Markovian processes can correspond to generative processes that are deterministic, giving rise to implicit models that produce high quality samples much faster. We empirically demonstrate that DDIMs can produce high quality samples 10 _×_ to 50 _×_ faster in terms of wall-clock time compared to DDPMs, allow us to trade off computation for sample quality, perform semantically meaningful image interpolation directly in the latent space, and reconstruct observations with very low error.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Deep generative models have demonstrated the ability to produce high quality samples in many domains (Karras et al., 2020; van den Oord et al., 2016a). In terms of image generation, generative adversarial networks (GANs, Goodfellow et al. (2014)) currently exhibits higher sample quality than likelihood-based methods such as variational autoencoders (Kingma & Welling, 2013), autoregressive models (van den Oord et al., 2016b) and normalizing flows (Rezende & Mohamed, 2015; Dinh et al., 2016). However, GANs require very specific choices in optimization and architectures in order to stabilize training (Arjovsky et al., 2017; Gulrajani et al., 2017; Karras et al., 2018; Brock et al., 2018), and could fail to cover modes of the data distribution (Zhao et al., 2018).

Recent works on iterative generative models (Bengio et al., 2014), such as denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPM, Ho et al. (2020)) and noise conditional score networks (NCSN, Song & Ermon (2019)) have demonstrated the ability to produce samples comparable to that of GANs, without having to perform adversarial training. To achieve this, many denoising autoencoding models are trained to denoise samples corrupted by various levels of Gaussian noise. Samples are then produced by a Markov chain which, starting from white noise, progressively denoises it into an image. This generative Markov Chain process is either based on Langevin dynamics (Song & Ermon, 2019) or obtained by reversing a forward _diffusion process_ that progressively turns an image into noise (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015).

A critical drawback of these models is that they require many iterations to produce a high quality sample. For DDPMs, this is because that the generative process (from noise to data) approximates the reverse of the forward _diffusion process_ (from data to noise), which could have thousands of steps; iterating over all the steps is required to produce a single sample, which is much slower compared to GANs, which only needs one pass through a network. For example, it takes around 20 hours to sample 50k images of size 32 _×_ 32 from a DDPM, but less than a minute to do so from a GAN on a Nvidia 2080 Ti GPU. This becomes more problematic for larger images as sampling 50k images of size 256 _×_ 256 could take nearly 1000 hours on the same GPU.

To close this efficiency gap between DDPMs and GANs, we present denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIMs). DDIMs are implicit probabilistic models (Mohamed & Lakshminarayanan, 2016) and are closely related to DDPMs, in the sense that they are trained with the same objective function.

In Section 3, we generalize the forward _diffusion process_ used by DDPMs, which is Markovian, to _non-Markovian_ ones, for which we are still able to design suitable reverse generative Markov chains. We show that the resulting variational training objectives have a shared surrogate objective, which is _exactly_ the objective used to train DDPM. Therefore, we can freely choose from a large family of generative models using the same neural network simply by choosing a different, _nonMarkovian_ diffusion process (Section 4.1) and the corresponding reverse generative Markov Chain. In particular, we are able to use _non-Markovian_ diffusion processes which lead to ”short” generative Markov chains (Section 4.2) that can be simulated in a small number of steps. This can massively increase sample efficiency only at a minor cost in sample quality.

In Section 5, we demonstrate several empirical benefits of DDIMs over DDPMs. _First_ , DDIMs have superior sample generation quality compared to DDPMs, when we accelerate sampling by 10 _×_ to 100 _×_ using our proposed method. _Second_ , DDIM samples have the following “consistency” property, which does not hold for DDPMs: if we start with the same initial latent variable and generate several samples with Markov chains of various lengths, these samples would have similar high-level features. _Third_ , because of “consistency” in DDIMs, we can perform semantically meaningful image interpolation by manipulating the initial latent variable in DDIMs, unlike DDPMs which interpolates near the image space due to the stochastic generative process.

## 2 BACKGROUND

Given samples from a data distribution _q_ ( _**x**_ 0), we are interested in learning a model distribution _pθ_ ( _**x**_ 0) that approximates _q_ ( _**x**_ 0) and is easy to sample from. Unlike typical latent variable models (such as the variational autoencoder (Rezende et al., 2014)), DDPMs are learned with a fixed (rather than trainable) inference procedure _q_ ( _**x**_ 1: _T |_ _**x**_ 0), and latent variables are relatively high dimensional. This is called the _forward process_ due to the autoregressive nature of the sampling procedure (from _**x**_ 0 to _**x** T_ ). We call the latent variable model _pθ_ ( _**x**_ 0: _T_ ), which is a Markov chain that samples from _**x** T_ to _**x**_ 0, the _generative process_ , since it approximates the intractable _reverse process q_ ( _**x** t−_ 1 _|_ _**x** t_ ). Intuitively, the forward process progressively adds noise to the observation _**x**_ 0, whereas the generative process progressively denoises a noisy observation (Figure 1, left).

(2020), the objective with _γ_ = **1** is optimized instead to maximize generation performance of the trained model; this is also the same objective used in noise conditional score networks (Song & Ermon, 2019) based on score matching (Hyv¨arinen, 2005; Vincent, 2011). From a trained model, _**x**_ 0 is sampled by first sampling _**x** T_ from the prior _pθ_ ( _**x** T_ ), and then sampling _**x** t−_ 1 from the generative processes iteratively.

The length _T_ of the forward process is an important hyperparameter in DDPMs. From a variational perspective, a large _T_ allows the reverse process to be close to a Gaussian (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015), so that the generative process modeled with Gaussian conditional distributions becomes a good approximation; this motivates the choice of large _T_ values, such as _T_ = 1000 in Ho et al. (2020). However, as all _T_ iterations have to be performed sequentially, instead of in parallel, to obtain a sample _**x**_ 0, sampling from DDPMs is much slower than sampling from other deep generative models, which makes them impractical for tasks where compute is limited and latency is critical.

## 3 VARIATIONAL INFERENCE FOR NON-MARKOVIAN FORWARD PROCESSES

Because the generative model approximates the reverse of the inference process, we need to rethink the inference process in order to reduce the number of iterations required by the generative model. Our key observation is that the DDPM objective in the form of _Lγ_ only depends on the marginals[2] _q_ ( _**x** t|_ _**x**_ 0), but not directly on the joint _q_ ( _**x**_ 1: _T |_ _**x**_ 0). Since there are many inference distributions (joints) with the same marginals, we explore alternative inference processes that are non-Markovian, which leads to new generative processes (Figure 1, right). These non-Markovian inference process lead to the same surrogate objective function as DDPM, as we will show below. In Appendix A, we show that the non-Markovian perspective also applies beyond the Gaussian case.

## 4 SAMPLING FROM GENERALIZED GENERATIVE PROCESSES

With _L_ **1** as the objective, we are not only learning a generative process for the Markovian inference process considered in Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015) and Ho et al. (2020), but also generative processes for many non-Markovian forward processes parametrized by _σ_ that we have described. Therefore, we can essentially use pretrained DDPM models as the solutions to the new objectives, and focus on finding a generative process that is better at producing samples subject to our needs by changing _σ_ .

We note another special case when _σt_ = 0 for all _t_[5] ; the forward process becomes deterministic given _**x** t−_ 1 and _**x**_ 0, except for _t_ = 1; in the generative process, the coefficient before the random noise _ϵt_ becomes zero. The resulting model becomes an implicit probabilistic model (Mohamed & Lakshminarayanan, 2016), where samples are generated from latent variables with a fixed procedure (from _**x** T_ to _**x**_ 0). We name this the _denoising diffusion implicit model_ (DDIM, pronounced /d:Im/), because it is an implicit probabilistic model trained with the DDPM objective (despite the forward process no longer being a diffusion).

## 4.2 ACCELERATED GENERATION PROCESSES

In the previous sections, the generative process is considered as the approximation to the reverse process; since of the forward process has _T_ steps, the generative process is also forced to sample _T_ steps. However, as the denoising objective _L_ **1** does not depend on the specific forward procedure as long as _qσ_ ( _**x** t|_ _**x**_ 0) is fixed, we may also consider forward processes with lengths smaller than _T_ , which accelerates the corresponding generative processes without having to train a different model.

Let us consider the forward process as defined not on all the latent variables _**x**_ 1: _T_ , but on a subset _{_ _**x** τ_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**x** τS }_ , where _τ_ is an increasing sub-sequence of [1 _, . . . , T_ ] of length _S_ . In particular, we define the sequential forward process over _**x** τ_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**x** τS_ such that _q_ ( _**x** τi|_ _**x**_ 0) = _N_ ( _[√] ατi_ _**x**_ 0 _,_ (1 _− ατi_ ) _**I**_ ) matches the “marginals” (see Figure 2 for an illustration). The generative process now samples latent variables according to reversed( _τ_ ), which we term _(sampling) trajectory_ . When the length of the sampling trajectory is much smaller than _T_ , we may achieve significant increases in computational efficiency due to the iterative nature of the sampling process.

Using a similar argument as in Section 3, we can justify using the model trained with the _L_ **1** objective, so no changes are needed in training. We show that only slight changes to the updates in Eq. (12) are needed to obtain the new, faster generative processes, which applies to DDPM, DDIM, as well as all generative processes considered in Eq. (10). We include these details in Appendix C.1.

In principle, this means that we can train a model with an arbitrary number of forward steps but only sample from some of them in the generative process. Therefore, the trained model could consider many more steps than what is considered in (Ho et al., 2020) or even a continuous time variable _t_ (Chen et al., 2020). We leave empirical investigations of this aspect as future work.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we show that DDIMs outperform DDPMs in terms of image generation when fewer iterations are considered, giving speed ups of 10 _×_ to 100 _×_ over the original DDPM generation process. Moreover, unlike DDPMs, once the initial latent variables _**x** T_ are fixed, DDIMs retain highlevel image features regardless of the generation trajectory, so they are able to perform interpolation directly from the latent space. DDIMs can also be used to encode samples that reconstruct them from the latent code, which DDPMs cannot do due to the stochastic sampling process.

For each dataset, we use the **same trained model** with _T_ = 1000 and the objective being _Lγ_ from Eq. (5) with _γ_ = **1** ; as we argued in Section 3, no changes are needed with regards to the training procedure. The only changes that we make is **how we produce samples from the model** ; we achieve this by controlling _τ_ (which controls how fast the samples are obtained) and _σ_ (which interpolates between the deterministic DDIM and the stochastic DDPM).

## 5.1 SAMPLE QUALITY AND EFFICIENCY

(2020) where DDIM is marginally worse. However, the sample quality of _σ_ ˆ becomes much worse for smaller dim( _τ_ ), which suggests that it is ill-suited for shorter trajectories. DDIM, on the other hand, achieves high sample quality much more consistently.

Even though DDPM could also achieve reasonable sample quality with 100 _×_ steps, DDIM requires much fewer steps to achieve this; on CelebA, the FID score of the 100 step DDPM is similar to that of the 20 step DDIM.

## 5.2 SAMPLE CONSISTENCY IN DDIMS

For DDIM, the generative process is deterministic, and _**x**_ 0 would depend only on the initial state _**x** T_ . In Figure 5, we observe the generated images under different generative trajectories (i.e. different _τ_ ) while starting with the same initial _**x** T_ . Interestingly, for the generated images with the same initial _**x** T_ , most high-level features are similar, regardless of the generative trajectory. In many cases, samples generated with only 20 steps are already very similar to ones generated with 1000 steps in terms of high-level features, with only minor differences in details. Therefore, it would appear that _**x** T_ alone would be an informative latent encoding of the image; and minor details that affects sample quality are encoded in the parameters, as longer sample trajectories gives better quality samples but do not significantly affect the high-level features. We show more samples in Appendix D.4.

- 5.3 INTERPOLATION IN DETERMINISTIC GENERATIVE PROCESSES

Figure 6: Interpolation of samples from DDIM with dim( _τ_ ) = 50.

Since the high level features of the DDIM sample is encoded by _**x** T_ , we are interested to see whether it would exhibit the semantic interpolation effect similar to that observed in other implicit probabilistic models, such as GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014). This is different from the interpolation procedure in Ho et al. (2020), since in DDPM the same _**x** T_ would lead to highly diverse _**x**_ 0 due to the stochastic generative process[6] . In Figure 6, we show that simple interpolations in _**x** T_ can lead to semantically meaningful interpolations between two samples. We include more details and samples in Appendix D.5. This allows DDIM to control the generated images on a high level directly through the latent variables, which DDPMs cannot.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Our work is based on a large family of existing methods on learning generative models as transition operators of Markov chains (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Bengio et al., 2014; Salimans et al., 2014; Song et al., 2017; Goyal et al., 2017; Levy et al., 2017). Among them, denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs, Ho et al. (2020)) and noise conditional score networks (NCSN, Song & Ermon (2019; 2020)) have recently achieved high sample quality comparable to GANs (Brock et al., 2018; Karras et al., 2018). DDPMs optimize a variational lower bound to the log-likelihood, whereas NCSNs optimize the score matching objective (Hyv¨arinen, 2005) over a nonparametric Parzen density estimator of the data (Vincent, 2011; Raphan & Simoncelli, 2011).

Despite their different motivations, DDPMs and NCSNs are closely related. Both use a denoising autoencoder objective for many noise levels, and both use a procedure similar to Langevin dynamics to produce samples (Neal et al., 2011). Since Langevin dynamics is a discretization of a gradient flow (Jordan et al., 1998), both DDPM and NCSN require many steps to achieve good sample quality. This aligns with the observation that DDPM and existing NCSN methods have trouble generating high-quality samples in a few iterations.

DDIM, on the other hand, is an implicit generative model (Mohamed & Lakshminarayanan, 2016) where samples are uniquely determined from the latent variables. Hence, DDIM has certain properties that resemble GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014) and invertible flows (Dinh et al., 2016), such as the ability to produce semantically meaningful interpolations. We derive DDIM from a purely variational perspective, where the restrictions of Langevin dynamics are not relevant; this could partially explain why we are able to observe superior sample quality compared to DDPM under fewer iterations. The sampling procedure of DDIM is also reminiscent of neural networks with continuous depth (Chen et al., 2018; Grathwohl et al., 2018), since the samples it produces from the same latent variable have similar high-level visual features, regardless of the specific sample trajectory.

## 7 DISCUSSION

We have presented DDIMs – an implicit generative model trained with denoising auto-encoding / score matching objectives – from a purely variational perspective. DDIM is able to generate highquality samples much more efficiently than existing DDPMs and NCSNs, with the ability to perform meaningful interpolations from the latent space. The non-Markovian forward process presented here seems to suggest continuous forward processes other than Gaussian (which cannot be done in the original diffusion framework, since Gaussian is the only stable distribution with finite variance). We also demonstrated a discrete case with a multinomial forward process in Appendix A, and it would be interesting to investigate similar alternatives for other combinatorial structures.

Moreover, since the sampling procedure of DDIMs is similar to that of an neural ODE, it would be interesting to see if methods that decrease the discretization error in ODEs, including multistep methods such as Adams-Bashforth (Butcher & Goodwin, 2008), could be helpful for further improving sample quality in fewer steps (Queiruga et al., 2020). It is also relevant to investigate whether DDIMs exhibit other properties of existing implicit models (Bau et al., 2019).

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This is a high-quality, seminal paper that addresses a critical bottleneck in diffusion-based generative modeling: the slow sampling speed caused by the Markovian requirement of DDPMs. The clarity of the writing is excellent, and the authors provide a compelling motivation by contrasting the high quality of diffusion models with the superior inference efficiency of GANs. The paper’s most significant contribution is the elegant theoretical reframing of the forward process as a non-Markovian distribution that still matches the DDPM marginals, thereby allowing for a deterministic, accelerated generative process without retraining the model. 

The primary strengths lie in the mathematical generalization and the resulting "consistency" property, which bridges the gap between probabilistic diffusion and deterministic implicit models. However, at this stage of the manuscript, a potential issue is the reliance on the assumption that the DDPM surrogate objective is the optimal way to train these non-Markovian processes; while empirically successful, the theoretical justification for why the surrogate remains the most effective bridge for $L_{simple}$ could be further explicated. Additionally, for a general academic audience, the distinction between the "implicit" nature of DDIM and the "probabilistic" nature of DDPM could benefit from more intuition regarding how the elimination of stochasticity at each step maintains the distribution’s integrity. Overall, this is a rigorous and impactful work that fundamentally changed the trajectory of generative AI research.

However, looking at the standard DDPM notation (Ho et al. 2020) which this paper builds upon, $\alpha_t$ usually refers to the cumulative product of $(1 - \beta_s)$, and indeed decreases toward zero. The issue arises in Section 3.1 (Equation 7), where the mean includes a term $\sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}$. If $\alpha_t$ is decreasing, then $\alpha_{t-1} > \alpha_t$. Specifically, Eq 3 defines the transition $q(\mathbf{x}_t | \mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ using $\alpha_t$ directly as the mean coefficient. In Ho et al., the transition uses $\sqrt{1-\beta_t}$, where $\prod (1-\beta_s) = \bar{\alpha}_t$. If $\alpha_t$ in Eq 3 refers to the marginal $\bar{\alpha}_t$, then the transition $q(\mathbf{x}_t | \mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ cannot be $\mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\alpha_t} \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \dots)$ because the product of those means would not result in $\sqrt{\alpha_t} \mathbf{x}_0$. While this makes $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ depend on both $\mathbf{x}_t$ and $\mathbf{x}_0$, the process is often still considered Markovian in the context of the joint distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_t | \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_0)$ if one views the state as the pair $(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0)$. The claim that these processes 'lead to the same training objective' is logically sound because the ELBO terms for diffusion models can be written as functions of marginals $q(\mathbf{x}_t | \mathbf{x}_0)$, but the distinction between the process being non-Markovian vs. the generative decomposition being non-standard is subtle.

Therefore, the term $\sqrt{1 - \alpha_{\tau_i}/\alpha_{\tau_{i-1}}}$ would involve taking the square root of a negative number if $\alpha_{\tau_i}$ (the later step) is smaller than $\alpha_{\tau_{i-1}}$ (the previous step in the forward chain). Usually, in DDIM/DDPM literature, the ratio is $\alpha_{t-1}/\alpha_t$ to ensure the value under the radical is positive. However, looking at the context of Eq 8 and Eq 15, the authors define $\sigma$ relative to the marginals. If the index $i$ refers to the generative direction (where $\tau_i$ is earlier in time than $\tau_{i-1}$), then the formula works; but the summary defines $\tau$ as an *increasing* sub-sequence. This creates a logical contradiction where the variance parameter would be undefined (square root of a negative) for the standard definition of $\alpha$.

comments.title: Ambiguous support for generative process when t=1
comments.quote: We add some Gaussian noise (with covariance $\sigma_1^2 \mathbf{I}$) for the case of $t=1$ to ensure that the generative process is supported everywhere.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors describe the generative process. They mention adding noise specifically at $t=1$. However, the core of the DDIM proposal (Section 4.1) is that $\sigma_t=0$ for all $t$ results in an implicit model. If $\sigma_1$ is forced to be non-zero to ensure global support, the model is not strictly deterministic/implicit at the final step. While this is a common trick to ensure density coverage in latent variable models, it slightly contradicts the claim in the Abstract and Section 4.1 that the process can be 'deterministic' and 'give rise to implicit models'. A careful reader might be confused whether the 'deterministic' claim excludes the very last step or if $\sigma_1 \to 0$ is assumed.

comments.title: Inconsistency in ODE limit reparameterization
comments.quote: $\frac{d\mathbf{x}(t)}{dt} = \epsilon_\theta\left(\frac{\mathbf{x}(t)}{\sqrt{\sigma^2+1}}, tight) \frac{d\sigma(t)}{dt}$
comments.explanation: In Section 4.3, the paper links DDIM to Neural ODEs. The term $\sigma$ is used here in a different context than the $\sigma_t$ variance parameters defined in Eq 7 and Eq 15. In the ODE context (and the summary's 'Key Equations'), $\sigma$ typically refers to the noise scale (e.g., $\sqrt{(1-\alpha)/\alpha}$). However, because the paper uses $\sigma$ primarily for the stochasticity hyperparameter (where $\sigma=0$ is DDIM), using the same Greek letter $\sigma$ for the ODE noise-level schedule in Section 4.3 is notationally confusing. A reader might try to plug $\sigma=0$ (the DDIM condition) into the ODE limit formula, which would yield a zero derivative, incorrectly implying the state never changes.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a significant and well-motivated contribution to the field of diffusion-based generative modeling by introducing Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIMs) as a faster, more flexible alternative to Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs). The writing is clear, logically structured, and effectively builds intuition through both theoretical analysis and visual aids such as Figure 1. The high-level idea—generalizing the Markovian diffusion framework of DDPMs to a non-Markovian setting while preserving the training objective—is both elegant and powerful, enabling substantial acceleration in sampling without sacrificing sample quality. The core theoretical insight, that multiple reverse processes can share the same training objective, is profound and opens up new design possibilities for iterative generative models. Empirically, the demonstrated speedups of 10× to 50× in wall-clock time are impressive and address one of the most critical limitations of DDPMs. However, a key issue is the abrupt truncation of the methodology section, which cuts off mid-sentence during the derivation of the variational objective, undermining the completeness and reproducibility of the technical contribution. This omission prevents full assessment of the mathematical rigor and may reflect poor manuscript preparation. Additionally, while the paper emphasizes the deterministic nature of DDIM’s generative process and its benefits for interpolation and consistency, it could better clarify the trade-offs involved—particularly how determinism affects sample diversity and whether mode coverage is impacted. Despite this, the work is innovative, clearly presented in the available portions, and has strong potential to influence subsequent research in efficient diffusion modeling. With full technical exposition, it would represent a high-quality, impactful contribution.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about sampling speed-up in abstract vs. empirical results
comments.quote: We empirically demonstrate that DDIMs can produce high quality samples 10 _×_ to 50 _×_ faster in terms of wall-clock time compared to DDPMs
comments.explanation: The abstract claims a speed-up of 10× to 50× in wall-clock time, but the paper's experiments use as few as 10 sampling steps versus 1000 in DDPMs, implying up to a 100× reduction in network evaluations. Since sampling time is dominated by the number of model calls, this suggests a potential 100× wall-clock improvement, especially given the authors’ own statement that DDPMs take ~20 hours vs. <1 minute for GANs on CIFAR-10. The upper bound in the abstract (50×) understates the maximum acceleration shown in Table 1 and Section 5.1. While the 10×–50× range may reflect conservative or averaged performance across configurations, the paper explicitly mentions ‘10 × to 100 ×’ in the introduction, creating internal inconsistency. The abstract should be revised to align with the broader claimed range for accuracy.

comments.title: Ambiguous and inconsistent definition of variance parameter σ in accelerated sampling
comments.quote: We consider different sub-sequences τ of [1 , . . . , T ] and different variance hyperparameters σ indexed by elements of τ . To simplify comparisons, we consider σ with the form: [η √(1−α_{τ_i}) / √(1−α_{τ_{i−1}})] where η ∈ R ≥ 0 is a hyperparameter that we can directly control.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces σ_t as a variance-related parameter in Section 3.1 but reuses it in Section 5 for subsequence τ without a clear redefinition. The parametrization σ_{τ_i} = η × √[(1−ᾱ_{τ_i})/(1−ᾱ_{τ_{i−1}})] × σ̂_{τ_i} (with σ̂_{τ_i} = √[1−ᾱ_{τ_i}/ᾱ_{τ_{i−1}}]) is introduced, yet the text omits this full form and instead states a simplified or corrupted version. This creates ambiguity about how η controls stochasticity. Furthermore, the conflation of η, σ̂, and σ in Table 1 and text leads to confusion—e.g., labeling η=1.0 and σ̂ separately despite both aiming to recover DDPM-like behavior. Crucially, the functional form of σ_t(η) is not clearly specified in the main text, only in appendix references, making reproduction difficult. The notation is further muddied by the sudden use of σ̂ and inconsistent presentation in summaries, risking misinterpretation of the interpolation mechanism between DDIM and DDPM.

comments.title: Incorrect or garbled expression for σ_t to recover DDPM
comments.quote: When _σt_ = (1 _− αt−_ 1) _/_ (1 _− αt_ ) ~~~~ 1 _− αt/αt−_ 1 for all _t_ , the forward process becomes Markovian, and the generative process becomes a DDPM.
comments.explanation: The formula for σ_t presented here appears corrupted—likely due to OCR—mixing a square root expression and a strikethrough, ending with the incorrect scalar expression '1 − αt/αt−1'. This is mathematically inconsistent with the known reverse variance in DDPMs. The correct condition should reflect that the generative process recovers DDPM when σ_t^2 corresponds to the standard DDPM reverse variance, typically involving (1−ᾱ_{t−1})/(1−ᾱ_t) and β̃_t = (1−ᾱ_{t−1})/(1−ᾱ_t) × (1−ᾱ_t)/ᾱ_t × ᾱ_{t−1}. The retained expression '1 − αt/αt−1' is incorrect and does not recover DDPM sampling. Even accounting for notational sloppiness, this could mislead readers attempting to implement or verify the equivalence. The intended expression needs clarification in context.

comments.title: Missing specification of σ_1 in the generative process
comments.quote: We add some Gaussian noise (with covariance _σ_ 1[2] **[I]**[) for the case of] _[ t][=][1][ to ensure that the generative process is] supported everywhere.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions adding noise with covariance σ_1²I at t=1 to ensure full support but fails to define how σ_1 is chosen. While σ_t is later parameterized via η in Section 5, no such parameterization is given at the point where σ_1 is introduced. This leaves the method underspecified: is σ_1 learned, fixed, or tied to other hyperparameters? Since this impacts reproducibility and could affect sampling stability, especially in deterministic limits (e.g., DDIM), the omission constitutes a meaningful gap. The lack of a clear rule for σ_1 creates ambiguity in implementation, even if the issue is resolved later via η-scaling.

comments.title: Misleading statement about the reverse process becoming deterministic when σ_t = 0
comments.quote: We note another special case when _σt_ = 0 for all _t_[5] ; the forward process becomes deterministic given _**x** t−_ 1 and _**x**_ 0, except for _t_ = 1
comments.explanation: This statement incorrectly refers to the 'forward process' becoming deterministic when σ_t = 0. In fact, σ_t controls the stochasticity in the *generative* (reverse) process. When σ_t = 0, the reverse transition q_σ(x_{t−1} | x_t, x_0) becomes deterministic, meaning x_{t−1} is fully determined by x_t and x_0—not the forward process. The phrasing misleadingly suggests that generating x_t is deterministic given earlier states, which contradicts the forward process definition. The correct interpretation—that the reverse step is deterministic—is recoverable from context, but the error in directionality (and use of 'forward process') could confuse readers new to diffusion models.

comments.title: Overstated and underspecified connection between DDIM and ODE integration
comments.quote: We can reparameterize ( √(1−α)/√α ) with λ and ( x / √α ) with H(λ) then sampling x_0 with Equation (13) can be treated as integration over the following ODE: [dH/dλ = ϕ_θ(H(λ), λ)]
comments.explanation: The paper claims that DDIM sampling 'can be treated as integration over' an ODE, drawing a connection to Neural ODEs. However, no derivation is provided, and the ODE is presented without justification or limiting assumptions. While it's known that deterministic samplers can resemble Euler integration of ODEs in the limit of small steps, this paper does not analyze convergence, define the time-reversed dynamics formally, or justify the sign and direction of the derivative (e.g., dH/dλ = −f_θ(H(λ))). The reparameterization suggests a suggestive analogy, particularly echoed in later work, but presenting it as factual without derivation or caveats overstates the analytical contribution. Given that the connection is used for interpretability and encoding insights (Section 4.3), it should be clearly labeled as a conceptual interpretation rather than a derived result.

comments.title: Misleading phrasing about DDPM benefits from large T due to reverse process being 'closer to Gaussian'
comments.quote: From a variational perspective, a large _T_ allows the reverse process to be close to a Gaussian (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015), so that the generative process modeled with Gaussian conditional distributions becomes a good approximation
comments.explanation: The claim that a large T makes the 'reverse process' closer to Gaussian is misleading. What actually happens is that with more steps, each forward step adds smaller noise, so the *reverse transition conditionals* p_θ(x_{t−1}|x_t) become closer to Gaussian, justifying the Gaussian assumption in the DDPM parameterization. The entire reverse process or latent path is not Gaussian. While the cited work (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015) supports this point, the phrasing risks misinterpretation—especially for new readers—who might assume the marginal dynamics are Gaussian. A more accurate statement would be that smaller time steps lead to better Gaussian approximations of individual transitions.

comments.title: Incorrect causal implication about sampling step count being forced to T
comments.quote: In the previous sections, the generative process is considered as the approximation to the reverse process; since of the forward process has _T_ steps, the generative process is also forced to sample _T_ steps.
comments.explanation: This statement uses the word 'forced', implying logical necessity that sampling must take T steps if the forward process has T steps. However, the paper’s central contribution is that marginal consistency allows *fewer* sampling steps (S < T) via non-Markovian inference. The claim would be accurate in a standard Markovian DDPM, but it appears *after* the framework has already been generalized with τ and σ, making the use of 'forced' incorrect in context. It undermines the paper’s own innovation. The intended meaning is likely that in the base case, sampling mirrors the forward schedule—but this should be clarified to avoid suggesting an inherent constraint.

comments.title: Ambiguous and undefined sampling trajectory indexing and boundary conditions
comments.quote: The generative process now samples latent variables according to reversed( τ ), which we term (sampling) trajectory .
comments.explanation: The term 'reversed(τ)' is used without formal definition. While interpretable as [τ_S, ..., τ_1], the starting point x_{τ_S} must be well-defined—typically x_T for the highest noise level. However, the paper does not require that τ_S = T, which is necessary for consistency unless additional imputation is used. This boundary condition is critical but left implicit. Readers may assume τ starts at T, but without explicit statement, implementations could vary. Given that trajectory selection is central to the speed-up claims, this ambiguity should be resolved.

comments.title: Inconsistent reporting and interpretation of FID results in text vs. table
comments.quote: DDIM ( η = 0) achieves the ˆ best sample quality when dim( τ ) is small, and DDPM ( η = 1 and σ ) typically has worse sample quality...
comments.explanation: The text claims DDIM (η=0) achieves the best quality for small dim(τ), which aligns with Table 1, but then refers to 'DDPM (η=1 and σ̂)' as a combined case, while the table lists η=1.0 and σ̂ as separate rows. This suggests σ̂ is an alternative variance setting (matching Ho et al. 2020 for CIFAR-10), but the conflation implies that η=1 and σ̂ are jointly applied, which is not supported by the table design. Moreover, σ̂ achieves better FID at 1000 steps (3.17) than DDIM (4.04), which could appear to contradict the claim that DDIM performs as well. However, this is reconciled by the fact that DDIM excels under acceleration (low S), while σ̂ degrades rapidly when S < 1000. The concern is not factual inaccuracy but presentation: the results and claims are contextually consistent but easy to misread without careful analysis. Improved clarification of what each row represents and how η and σ̂ relate would prevent misunderstanding.

comments.title: Redundant and garbled notation for the set of denoising functions ϕ_θ
comments.quote: where _ϕθ_ := _{ϕ_[(] _θ[t]_[)] _[}] t[T]_ =1[is a set of] _[ T]_[functions, each] _[ ϕ]_[(] _θ[t]_[)] : _X →X_ (indexed by _t_ ) is a function with trainable parameters _θ_[(] _[t]_[)]
comments.explanation: The notation defining ϕ_θ is syntactically broken, likely due to OCR, with duplicated and misplaced brackets and phrasing (e.g., 'each] [ ϕ]_[(] θ[t]_[)]'). The intended meaning—that ϕ_θ is a collection of T separate denoising networks {ϕ_θ[t]}_{t=1}^T—is standard and clear in context, especially given the non-shared parameter assumption. However, the phrasing is redundant and awkward even absent corruption. While not a technical flaw, the lack of clean exposition adds minor friction to understanding. The issue is primarily presentational.

comments.title: Unjustified claim that surrogate training objective is 'exactly' the DDPM objective
comments.quote: We show that the resulting variational training objectives have a shared surrogate objective, which is _exactly_ the objective used to train DDPM.
comments.explanation: While technically correct under parameter independence (Theorem 1), using the word 'exactly' may mislead readers into thinking the full evidence lower bound (ELBO) is identical across different σ. In reality, the ELBO depends on the inference process q_σ, but the *surrogate* L_γ (or L_1) is shared and sufficient to train all models. The practical implication—that existing DDPM training code can be reused—is valid. However, the strong claim of 'exactly' risks oversimplification, suggesting identical probabilistic models rather than shared optimization objectives. A more precise phrasing (e.g., 'the same practical training objective') would better reflect the nuance and prevent misinterpretation, especially by practitioners.

This omission prevents full assessment of the mathematical rigor and may reflect poor manuscript preparation.
